Surprising fact: Born when a salesman asked KY coal miners what they wanted and one framed the moon: 'as big as that.' ~1M made/day, still independent.
Named for a real granddaughter, age 4 at launch (1960); still family-owned. Also owns Drake's, Sunbelt.
Named 'Double' because the bottle was twice rivals'; pioneered the soda industry's first Applied Color Label.
Built insuring railroad workers; offered the first disability benefits (1939); marked 175 years in 2023.
Started by a newspaper publisher (Roy McDonald); its Cameron Hill campus is the largest LEED-Gold campus in TN.
Funded by founder Dr. J. Don Brock's royalties from a carpet-dryer invention — now ~$1.5B in sales.
A huge share of the planet's tow trucks (Century, Vulcan, Chevron, Holmes) are built in a Chattanooga suburb.

Spun out of Access America; among the fastest-growing 3PLs in the U.S.
The only insurance agency named to the FreightTech 100 every year since its inception.
First gig aimed for ~30 dorm moves at Auburn orientation and did 325; now in 65+ cities.
Same name lineage as the fleet-payments firm Craig Fuller sold to U.S. Bank — part of the Fuller ecosystem.
Born from the Access America windfall; backed unicorns Sennder and Stord.
Beat Google to gigabit (and to 10-Gig in 2015, 25-Gig in 2022) — built over a Comcast lawsuit.

A ~century-old family business quietly makes much of the bakery/to-go packaging behind American food.
Anchored the city's outdoor identity; ownership has since changed hands (verify current owner).
Owned by Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway since 2001 — the world's biggest carpet company sits in a small GA town.
The two largest flooring companies on Earth — Shaw and Mohawk — are headquartered ~30 miles apart.
Founded by Bob Shaw — who'd already built Shaw Industries — making him a two-time carpet titan after 'retiring.'
Began as Dixie Mercerizing, treating yarn for ladies' stockings, then pivoted to luxury carpet.

Connection: Founded Decatur IL 1857; water-products HQ → Chattanooga 2010
Surprising fact: A large share of America's fire hydrants now trace through Chattanooga. (HQ here now, not born here.)
Two local lawyers bought near-nationwide bottling rights from Asa Candler for ~$1 — he thought bottling was a fad. The most lucrative handshake in beverage history.

Iconic for: Gold Bond, Icy Hot, Selsun Blue, Pamprin
Connection: Founded Chattanooga 1879; acquired by Sanofi 2010 (now Opella)
Surprising fact: Gold Bond & Icy Hot are STILL made in Chattanooga (Opella's only U.S. plant). In WWII it was a top maker of K-rations.

Iconic for: The square steamed slider; South's oldest fast-food chain
Surprising fact: 2nd-oldest fast-food chain in America; named for how 'crystal clean' the first diner looked. Ch. 11 in 2020.

Iconic for: One of the largest U.S. truckload carriers

Lost to a corporate roll-up, then brought back by the founders' grandson; now under Vienna Coffee (Maryville).
Raised $24M (2017) for 'codeless' app development.

The REAL local Buster Brown — apparel, not shoes. (The footwear trademark is Brown Shoe of St. Louis — a common myth.)

Connection: Founded Tuscaloosa AL 1933; HQ Chattanooga 1941; → Lifetouch 2011
Surprising fact: Ran one of the largest portrait operations in the U.S. (and UK) from Chattanooga for 70 years. (HQ here, not born here.)

Connection: Founded 2015 (Chattanooga + Des Moines); acquired by WorkStep 2025
Surprising fact: Dynamo-backed; clients included Covenant.

Connection: Incubated at Chattanooga's Brickyard (2021); acquired by NVIDIA 2024
Surprising fact: Brickyard's first exit — incubated here, though its founders were not local.

Chattanooga landed Japan's first U.S. heavy-equipment plant in 1985 — and the city's first Japanese plant.
